coredata.dc:description: The currently reported carbon dots with oxidase-like activity, as substitutes for natural enzymes, are generally prepared using organics with small molecules as carbon precursors. However, renewable cellulose as a feedstock is more conducive to the sustainable production of carbon dots. Herein, the Mn and N elements doped carbon dots with oxidase-like activity were prepared from carboxymethylcellulose, ethylenediamine, and MnCl2, and the effects of these three elements on the structure of the carbon dots were comprehensively studied and verified by advanced techniques. Moreover, the increase of ethylenediamine dosage and the prolongation of hydrothermal time promoted the coordination of O-based and N-based groups to Mn ions. The enhancement of the Mn-N and Mn-O bond content strengthened the oxidation-like activity of the carbon dots. The maximum reaction velocity and the Michaelis constant for 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine were 19.8 × 10-5 mM s−1 and 0.159 mM, respectively. Finally, a platform for the dual-mode detection of nitrite was established based on the catalytic ability of the carbon dots for 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine and the diazotization reaction of 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine with nitrite. The response ranges of ratiometric colorimetric and fluorescence detection for nitrite were 0–200 μM and 0–100 μM, respectively. This work verified the feasibility of constructing carbon dots with oxidase-like activity and fluorescence property from carboxymethylcellulose, which can stimulate more efforts to explore the application of biomass-based carbon dots with oxidase-like activity in the field of nitrite detection.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The currently reported carbon dots with oxidase-like activity, as substitutes for natural enzymes, are generally prepared using organics with small molecules as carbon precursors. However, renewable cellulose as a feedstock is more conducive to the sustainable production of carbon dots. Herein, the Mn and N elements doped carbon dots with oxidase-like activity were prepared from carboxymethylcellulose, ethylenediamine, and MnCl2, and the effects of these three elements on the structure of the carbon dots were comprehensively studied and verified by advanced techniques. Moreover, the increase of ethylenediamine dosage and the prolongation of hydrothermal time promoted the coordination of O-based and N-based groups to Mn ions. The enhancement of the Mn-N and Mn-O bond content strengthened the oxidation-like activity of the carbon dots. The maximum reaction velocity and the Michaelis constant for 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine were 19.8 × 10-5 mM s−1 and 0.159 mM, respectively. Finally, a platform for the dual-mode detection of nitrite was established based on the catalytic ability of the carbon dots for 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine and the diazotization reaction of 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine with nitrite. The response ranges of ratiometric colorimetric and fluorescence detection for nitrite were 0–200 μM and 0–100 μM, respectively. This work verified the feasibility of constructing carbon dots with oxidase-like activity and fluorescence property from carboxymethylcellulose, which can stimulate more efforts to explore the application of biomass-based carbon dots with oxidase-like activity in the field of nitrite detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanozymes, as natural enzyme substitutes, are widely applied in sensing detection, antibacterial, and cancer treatment applications due to their high chemical stability, low production cost, and tunable catalytic activity (Hamed et al., 2024; Singh et al., 2024). At present, nanozymes mainly include metal–organic frameworks, metal single-atoms, metal oxides, and carbon materials (Feng et al., 2023; Vyas and Indra, 2024; Zhang et al., 2022). Carbon dots (CDs), a type of nanomaterial with sizes less than 10 nm, typically have excellent photoluminescent properties. Meanwhile, CDs can initiate enzyme-like activity through structural design and elemental doping (Gan et al., 2023; Kong and Zhou, 2024). The combination of enzyme-like activity and photoluminescent property opens up new potential pathways for the application of CDs in biomedicine (anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and cancer therapeutics) and multimodal sensing detection (Rui et al., 2023).

The current studies of CDs-derived nanozymes have focused on their peroxidase-like activity (Li et al., 2022). Generally, the unstable H2O2 is required to be added into the detecting system when such CDs are applied as sensors, which may lead to some application limitations. For example, H2O2 is needed to be stored away from light and at low temperatures (Yang et al., 2024; Yin et al., 2024). As another member of the nanozymes family, oxidase-like CDs only require dissolved oxygen in solution to catalyze the oxidation of substrates, which makes their detection process more convenient and securer (Zhuo et al., 2019). In this regard, some nitrogen (e.g., ethylenediamine, dopamine) and metal dopants (e.g., MnCl2, CuCl2, FeCl2) have been introduced in the process of preparing CDs (Purbia et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2024). In such CDs, metal dopants can endow CDs with catalytic active sites, while nitrogen dopants can modulate their catalytic activity and fluorescence properties simultaneously. In this respect, Wang et al. prepared Mn, N-CDs using citric acid, ethylenediamine (EDA), and MnCl2 and promoted their oxidase-like activity by increasing the dosage of EDA (Wang et al., 2022). However, the reason for the nitrogen dopants affecting the oxidase-like activity of CDs was unclear, meanwhile, the studies relating to CDs with oxidase-like activity were scarcely reported.

Moreover, the carbon sources used to prepare oxidase-like CDs are generally from synthesized organics with small molecules, such as citric acid, serine, and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) (Kong and Zhou, 2024). However, the growing demand for carbon neutrality and sustainable development has driven the need to exploit renewable carbon precursors to produce CDs (Hou et al., 2023; Song et al., 2022). Cellulose, as the most abundant component of biomass, can be extracted from non-food agricultural and forestry wastes, such as wood, straw, and corn cobs (Gan et al., 2023). To date, many researchers have studied the photoluminescence properties of cellulose-based CDs. However, compared to organics with small molecules, the polymeric structure of cellulose undergoes more complex and stochastic reactions during CDs preparation process (Gan et al., 2023). Therefore, it is worthwhile to fabricate the oxidase-like activity of CDs using cellulose as a sustainable precursor.

Nitrite is a commonly used additive for meat preservation, as it can inhibit the growth of microorganisms (Lin et al., 2018). However, excessive intake of nitrites could cause serious health hazards such as poisoning and cancer (Guo et al., 2023a). Currently, some detection methods for nitrite have been established, such as ion chromatography (Doyle et al., 2000), gas-phase chemiluminescence (Cox and Frank, 1982), and Griess reagent (Nussler et al., 2006). However, these methods usually suffer from high analytical costs and complicated operation procedures (Wang et al., 2017). Additionally, fluorescence or ratiometric colorimetric methods are applied to detect nitrite (Lin et al., 2018), (Peng et al., 2023a). However, the single detection modes are susceptible to environmental interference, such as the color or fluorescence of samples (Tao et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022). Fortunately, based on oxidase-like activity and fluorescence properties, CDs can be applied to build ratiometric colorimetric/fluorescence multimodal sensing platforms to detect nitrite.

The primary novelty of this work was to explore, for the first time, the feasibility of preparing CDs with oxidase-like activity and fluorescence properties from a cellulose derivative. Herein, MnNCD was prepared from carboxymethylcellulose sodium (CMC), ethylenediamine (EDA), and MnCl2 via hydrothermal reaction (Scheme 1 ). The roles of CMC, EDA, and MnCl2 on MnNCD properties and structure were explored. Moreover, the impacts of the EDA dosage and hydrothermal time on the oxidase-like activity of MnNCD were investigated. The steady-state kinetic results showed that MnNCD2-36, as the best sample, had a fast response (Vmax = 19.80 × 10-5 mM s−1) and high sensitivity (Km = 0.159 mM) to 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine. Thereafter, based on the oxidase-like activity and fluorescence property of MnNCD2-36, the fluorescence/ratiometric dual-mode assay system for the detection of nitrite was established (Scheme 1). This work provided a feasible way for the preparation of cellulose-based oxidase-like CDs, which contributed to improving the high-value application of cellulose and inspiring the exploration of more sustainable CD-derived nanozymes and their applications in biochemical sensing in the future.

Carboxymethylcellulose sodium (CMC), 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), NaNO2, NaH2PO4·2H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine (EDA, 99 %), ethanol, sodium acetate (NaAc), acetic acid (HAc), MnCl2·4H2O, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, Na2SO4, CuCl2, NaHCO3, KCl were purchased from Kermel Analytical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). All chemicals were used as received. A dialysis tube (pore size of 1000 Da) was purchased from Viskase (USA). The 100 mL para polyphenylene (PPL) −lined autoclave was purchased from Xian Hongchen Instrument Co., Ltd (Xian, China). The luncheon meat, sausage, and salted duck eggs were purchased from local supermarkets (Dalian, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Production and characterization of CDs doped with N, Mn elements (MnNCD)
ce:section.ce:para: Mn and N elements carbon dots (MnNCD) were prepared using a hydrothermal treatment procedure. In detail, 2 g of CMC, 2 g of MnCl2·4H2O, and specific amounts (1 g, 2 g, or 3 g) of EDA were dispersed in 50 mL of deionized water (DW). After stirring for 30 min, the mixtures were transferred into a PPL-lined autoclave and heated at 220°C for predetermined hours (12 h, 24 h, or 36 h). After cooling to room temperature, the resulting solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter. The filtrates were then dialyzed against DW in a dialysis tube for 72 h, and the purified products were finally dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C. The MnNCD samples prepared with different amounts of EDA and hydrothermal treatment time were denoted as MnNCDx-y (x was the amount of EDA, y was the hydrothermal time). To study the roles of CMC, EDA, and MnCl2 during the hydrothermal process, the NCD, MnCD, and MnN were prepared using CMC and EDA, CMC and MnCl2, and EDA and MnCl2 as raw materials, respectively. The detailed preparation conditions are shown in Table S1 (supplementary material).

The FTIR spectra of the samples were generated by a PerkinElmer Fourier I spectrophotometer (USA). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of samples were generated on a JEM-2100 microscope (Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the samples was conducted using a Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB250Xi (USA) instrument. The fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan). The quantum yield (QY) of the CDs was calculated with quinine sulfate in H2SO4 solution (0.1 M) as a standard (Zhao et al., 2021) and calculated using equation (1):

The UV–Vis absorbance spectra of the samples were measured on a Hitachi UH-5300 spectrophotometer (Japan). The radical trapping of the samples was conducted using a Bruker A3000-10/12 electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrometer. The XRD spectra of the samples were collected using a Shimadzu 6100 X-ray diffractometer (Japan). The interlayer spacing (d002) was calculated based on the Bragg equation (2):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidase-like activity and steady-state kinetics of MnNCD
ce:section.ce:para:
To determine the oxidase-like activity of MnNCD, 50 μL of MnNCD (1 mg/mL) and 100 μL of TMB (5 mM) were added to 2850 μL of NaAc-HAc buffer solution (pH = 4, 0.2 M). After mixing for 2 min, the UV–Vis absorbance of the mixtures was measured at 652 nm using a MACYLAB spectrophotometer (UV-1200PC, China). The oxidase-like activity of MnNCD was assessed based on the absorbance. According to the above method and using TMB as the substrate, the steady-state kinetics of MnNCD were investigated by varying the concentration of TMB. The kinetic constants of the oxidase-like activity were calculated based on the Michaelis-Menten equation (3):
(3)

where V and Vmax are the initial velocity and maximum reaction velocity, respectively, [S] and Km are the substrate concentration and the Michaelis constant. The absorbance of a peak for the TMB oxidation product (TMB+) at 652 nm can be correlated with the concentration of TMB+ according to Beer-Lambert Law (4):
(4)
A = ε b c
where A is the absorbance of TMB+ at 652 nm, b is the optical length of 1.0 cm, ε is the molar absorption coefficient of 39000 M/cm, and c is the concentration for TMB+

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric colorimetric/fluorescence dual-mode detection of nitrite
ce:section.ce:para: In this set of experiments, the nitrite content of solutions was determined by MnNCD and TMB mixed solution. In detail, 25 µL of MnNCD (1 mg/mL), and 50 µL of TMB (5 mM) were added to 2875 µL of NaAC-HAc solution (pH = 4, 0.2 M). After mixing for 2 min, 50 µL of nitrite solution at different concentrations (0–300 µM) was added to the above system. Incubation was prolonged for another 15 min, and the fluorescence intensity and UV–Vis absorption spectra of the resulting solutions were measured.

In order to verify the practicability of the MnNCD and TMB mixed solution system for nitrite detection, luncheon meat, sausage, and salted duck eggs were pretreated according to previous methods (Peng et al., 2023b). The luncheon meat, sausage, and salted duck eggs were stirred with a blender and then sonicated in a water bath at 75 °C for 5 min. The induced samples were cooled to room temperature, and then 15 mL of ethanol was added to the samples. The mixtures were then transferred to a 50 mL volumetric flask and diluted with deionized water. Subsequently, the solution was centrifuged and filtered through a 0.22 µm microfiltration membrane. Finally, the supernatant was added to different concentrations of standard nitrite solutions and determined by MnNCD and TMB mixed solution according to the method illustrated in section 2.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The roles of CMC, EDA, and MnCl2 in the preparation of MnNCD
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of MnNCD was illustrated in Fig. 1 a. Using the renewable and low-cost cellulose derivative (CMC) as a carbon source and ethylenediamine (EDA) and MnCl2 as dopants, MnNCD was prepared via the facile one-step hydrothermal method. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1b) indicated that the obtained MnNCD2-24 were nearly spherical nanoparticles with particle sizes close to 4 nm and had good dispersibility. Subsequently, NCD, MnCD, and MnN were prepared to investigate the roles of CMC, EDA, and MnCl2 on the properties (fluorescent properties and oxidase-like activity) and structure of MnNCD2-24 and to elucidate the association between the properties and structure of MnNCD2-24.

Fluorescence was an intrinsic property of CDs, which can be evaluated based on the quantum yield (QY) (Fig. 1c). The NCD and MnNCD2-24 exhibited excellent fluorescence properties (QY = 21 % and 22 %), which were much higher than those of the MnCD and MnN (QY = 4.0 % and 4.9 %). The corresponding fluorescence images are shown in Fig. 1e. The NCD and MnNCD2-24 solutions emitted bright blue fluorescence under UV light, while the fluorescence of the MnCD and MnN solutions was negligible. Subsequently, 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), a classical chromogenic substrate, was incubated with different samples to assess their oxidase-like activity. This was because colorless TMB can be catalyzed into blue TMB+ by CDs with oxidase-like activity, which displayed a distinct UV–Vis absorption signal at 652 nm (Wu et al., 2024a). The oxidase-like activity of CDs could be evaluated according to the color of the solution and the UV–Vis absorbance value at 652 nm (Zhang et al., 2024). Fig. 1d exhibits the UV–Vis absorbance of the four CDs at 652 nm. The absorbance of MnNCD2-24 and MnN were 0.23 and 0.81, respectively, and the corresponding solutions changed color from colorless to blue-green (Fig. 1f), indicating that TMB was catalytically oxidized to TMB+. On the contrary, the UV–Vis absorbance of NCD and MnCD at 652 nm was negligible, indicating that they did not possess oxidase-like activity. According to such results, MnNCD2-24 showed both excellent fluorescence property and oxidase-like activity, which confirmed that cellulose-derived materials can be used as feedstock for CDs with oxidase-like activity. In addition, the effects of CMC, EDA, and MnCl2 on the fluorescence property and oxidase-like activities of MnNCD2-24 were analyzed. In conclusion, EDA and MnCl2 were indispensable for the formation of MnNCD2-24 with oxidase-like activities, while the addition of CMC and EDA was essential for the fluorescence property of MnNCD2-24. Subsequently, the relationship between the structure and properties of MnNCD2-24 was further explored.

The XRD pattern (Fig. 1g) of NCD displayed a broad peak at 2θ of 23.5°, corresponding to the graphitic carbon (002) crystal plane (Bao et al., 2015), which was significantly weakened in the XRD pattern of MnNCD2-24, indicating that the doping of Mn elements could disrupt the graphitic structure of CDs. Unexpectedly, the XRD pattern of MnN showed diffraction peaks corresponding to Mn3O4 (Li et al., 2019). The diffraction peaks of Mn3O4 were not found in the patterns of the other three samples, which could be attributed to the following two reasons: 1) the content of Mn element in the MnNCD2-24, and MnCD was so low that the diffraction peaks corresponding to metal oxides could not be detected, and 2) the polymer framework of CMC and the coordination of CMC with Mn2+ contributed to a better dispersion of Mn species during hydrothermal process, which facilitated the binding of Mn species with the O-based and N-based functional groups.

The surface chemical compositions of these four samples were analyzed by XPS spectra (Fig. 2 and Fig. S2). The content of Mn element in MnCD, MnN, and MnNCD2-24 was 2.31 %, 2.75 %, and 0.4 %, respectively. The result showed that the content of Mn element in MnCD was similar to that in MnN. Although MnCD contained a high content of Mn, no diffraction peaks of manganese oxides appeared in the XRD spectrum of MnCD, which proved the well-dispersion of Mn species by CMC. In addition, the content of Mn element in MnNCD2-24 was only 0.4 %, which was probably due to the addition of EDA affecting the coordination of CMC with Mn ions. Nevertheless, the oxidase-like activity of MnNCD2-24 indicated that the N-coordination environment provided by EDA was essential for the catalytic effect of Mn species. The content of C and O elements in NCD and MnNCD2-24 was 71.80 %, 19.98 %, and 72.69 %, 15.93 %, demonstrating that the preparation of MnNCD2-24 underwent a more intense deoxygenation reaction. The lower content of C element in MnCD (70.29 %) than in MnNCD2-24 (72.69 %) could be explained by the absence of EDA in the preparation process of CDs, which generated a relatively mild reaction process.

In order to better understand the coordination environment of the Mn active centers, the bonding information of the samples was further analyzed by high-resolution spectra of XPS (Fig. 2). The high-resolution spectra of C 1 s for these four samples were deconvoluted at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, 287.9 eV, and 288.8 eV into CC/CC, CO/CN, CO, and COOH, respectively. The O 1 s high-resolution spectrum of the NCD demonstrated the bonding information of CO (531.05 eV), CO (532.37 eV), and C-OH (533.38 eV). Different from NCD, the high-resolution spectra of O 1 s of MnNCD2-24, MnCD, and MnN displayed Mn-O signals at 529.13 eV, 530.29 eV, and 529.78 eV, respectively, with other CO, CO, and C-OH signals (Cheng et al., 2022). Among them, MnN had much higher Mn-O content than MnNCD2-24, which was consistent with the results of oxidase-like activity. MnCD did not exhibit oxidase-like activity, although it had a high content of Mn-O bonds. The high-resolution spectra of N 1 s might explain this phenomenon. For NCD, the peaks at 399.65 eV, 398.76 eV, 400.24 eV, and 401.66 eV are attributed to pyridine N, amino N, pyrrole N, and graphite N, respectively (Zhao et al., 2021). Upon the introduction of Mn elements, the MnNCD2-24 showed a peak at 398.80 eV, corresponding to the Mn-N bond (Chen et al., 2020). Similarly, the high-resolution spectrum of N 1 s of MnN confirmed the presence of Mn-N (399.05 eV) bonds. However, MnCD prepared only from CMC, and MnCl2 lacked the Mn-N bond, which might be the reason for its lack of oxidase-like activity. Therefore, it can be hypothesized that oxidase-like activity only occurred when Mn species were coordinated with both O-based and N-based functional groups. As shown in Fig. 2e-g, the high-resolution spectra of Mn 2p of MnNCD2-24, MnCD, and MnN could be deconvoluted into Mn2+ and Mn3+ and associated shake-up peaks, demonstrating that the oxidation state of Mn was a mixture of Mn2+ and Mn3+ states (Singh et al., 2023).

The FTIR spectra (Fig. 1h) were used to analyze the chemical structure of CDs furtherly. In the FTIR spectrum of CMC, the broad peaks at 3200–3600 cm−1 originate from the OH stretching vibration, and the peaks at 1600 cm−1 and 1424 cm−1 are attributing to the carboxymethyl functional groups (Abdullaevich et al., 2022). The broad peaks at 3200 and 3400 cm−1 in the spectra of NCD and MnNCD2-24 correspond to OH and NH stretching vibrations, respectively (Zhu et al., 2022). The peaks at 1645 cm−1 and 1385 cm−1 are assigned to the N-CO and CN stretching vibrations, respectively (Shi et al., 2022). The results indicated the successful incorporation of N elements in the CDs. Different from other samples, the FTIR spectrum of MnN showed sharp peaks at 501 cm−1 and 614 cm−1, indicating the presence of manganese oxides (Kuthati et al., 2019), consistent with the XRD analysis.

One of the advantages of nanomaterials was their excellent dispersion in aqueous solutions, which gave them an edge in liquid sensing applications. As shown in Fig. 1i, the Zeta potentials of NCD, MnNCD2-24, MnCD, and MnN were 5 mV, 27 mV, −26 mV, and –23 mV, respectively. The larger absolute value of Zeta potential would generate greater electrostatic repulsion between the particles, which was conducive to the stable dispersion of particles in aqueous solution (Shi et al., 2022). The optical picture in Fig. S1 illustrated that MnNCD2-24 and MnCD had better dispersion than NCD and MnN within the time frame of this study. The excellent dispersion of the samples in solution protected them from aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) and contributed to the stability of their fluorescence property and oxidase-like activity during storage. Moreover, although MnN had a significant negative potential, the stability of MnN was worse than that of other samples due to the easy aggregation of manganese oxides in aqueous solution (Fu and Xie, 2020; Grza̧dka et al., 2014; Huangfu et al., 2013). The results again demonstrated the importance of introducing CMC to disperse Mn species in the preparation of CDs.

Based on all the above analyses, the main structure and groups of MnNCD can be proposed (Fig. 1a). In addition to the carbon skeleton, MnNCD had abundant oxygen-containing groups (e.g., carboxyl, hydroxyl) and N-containing groups (e.g., amino, amide). Meanwhile, Mn-N and Mn-O bonds formed by the coordination of O atoms and N atoms with Mn atoms were also present in the structure of MnNCD. This structure of MnNCD was similar to that of CDs with oxidase-like activity reported in the previous literature (Wang et al., 2024, 2022). Moreover, the roles of CMC, EDA, and MnCl2 during the preparation process of MnNCD were elucidated. As carbon precursors, CMC and its depolymerization products could form the backbone of the CDs and provide oxygen-containing functional groups to react with EDA and Mn elements. EDA could provide an alkaline environment and abundant N elements during the hydrothermal process. The alkaline environment was beneficial to the depolymerization of CMC into organic acids (Yan and Qi, 2014). The N atoms could promote the n − π* transition ability of CDs and thus increase their quantum yields. At the same time, the N atoms could coordinate with the Mn atoms to produce oxidase-like activity in CDs. The MnCl2 provided Mn atoms for CDs to form the catalytic center.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of EDA and reaction time on the performance of MnNCD
ce:section.ce:para: The introduction of EDA during the MnNCD preparation process was critical for the generation of oxidase-like activity due to its influence on the coordination environment of Mn elements. However, the mechanism of EDA and Mn species coordination was still unknown, which was not conducive to the establishment of regulatory strategies for oxidase-like activity. Therefore, the effects of different amounts (1 g, 2 g, or 3 g) of EDA on the oxidase-like activity of MnNCD were explored to improve the understanding of the oxidase-like activity of MnNCD. Fig. 3 a and Fig. S3a demonstrate the oxidase-like activity and fluorescence intensity of MnNCD. The catalytic ability of MnNCD gradually improved with the increase in EDA usage, but their fluorescence intensity gradually decreased, which was consistent with the previous literature (Wang et al., 2022). The XRD patterns of all MnNCD (Fig. 3b) showed a broad peak corresponding to the graphitic carbon (002) crystal plane. However, the (002) diffraction peak gradually shifted towards 2θ = 26° with increasing EDA amounts. The calculated d002 for MnNCD1-24, MnNCD2-24, and MnNCD3-24 were 0.370 nm, 0.356 nm, and 0.342 nm, respectively. The decrease in graphite layer spacing (d002) for these three CDs suggested that increasing the EDA amounts could promote the hydrothermal reaction performed under a harsher condition.

As shown in Fig. 4 , the content of O element in MnNCD1-24, MnNCD2-24, and MnNCD3-24 was gradually increased. This increase was because the increase in the EDA usage would raise the pH value of the hydrothermal process and thus facilitate the conversion of the CMC to organic acids (Yin et al., 2011). Interestingly, there was no significant difference in the content of the N element in these three CDs, which were 11.54 %, 10.98 %, and 11.22 %, respectively. The results suggested that the lowest content of EDA could provide sufficient amino groups for the hydrothermal process. Therefore, the amount of N doping in MnNCD was not the main factor affecting their oxidase-like activity. The content of Mn element in MnNCD1-24, MnNCD2-24, and MnNCD3-24 was 0.68 %, 0.40 %, and 0.78 %, respectively, which was inconsistent with the trend of oxidase-like activity. However, the catalytic capacity of Mn, N-CD was not directly related to the Mn element content. It was mainly influenced by the coordination environment of the Mn species.

Subsequently, the coordination environment of the Mn species was further discussed through high-resolution elemental spectra of XPS (Fig. 4 and Fig. S4). Mn-N signals were found in the high-resolution spectra of N 1 s of all samples, but Mn-O signals were only shown in the high-resolution spectra of O 1 s of MnNCD2-24 and MnNCD3-24 samples. The absence of the Mn-O bond in MnNCD1-24 was possible due to that the reaction of EDA with the carboxyl group interfered with the coordination of the O-type functional group and the Mn species. The results of oxidase-like activities and coordination environments of Mn species suggested that Mn-N or Mn-O bonds alone could not impart excellent catalytic capacity to MnNCD. As the EDA usage increased, more organic acids and harsher reaction environments promoted Mn-O generation. The percentages of Mn-O in MnNCD2-24 and MnNCD3-24 were 3.09 % and 9.51 %, respectively, and the percentages of Mn-N in them were 2.94 % and 5.72 %, respectively (Fig. 4b and 4c). The higher Mn-O and Mn-N content in MnNCD3-24 was consistent with their optimal catalytic ability. The high-resolution spectrum of Mn 2p demonstrated that the oxidation state of Mn was a mixture of Mn2+ (640.8 and 652.2 eV) and Mn3+(642.7 and 653.6 eV) (Han et al., 2016).

The peaks appearing in FTIR spectra (Fig. 3c) at 3400 cm−1 and 3200 cm−1 are assigned to OH and NH stretching vibrations, respectively (Zhu et al., 2022). The peaks at 1645 cm−1 and 1385 cm−1 proved the presence of the amide bond and CN, respectively (Shi et al., 2022). In addition, the peak at 545 cm−1 for MnNCD2-24 and MnNCD3-24 is assigned to Mn-N/Mn-O (Wang et al., 2024), while MnNCD1-24 had only a weak peak at 545 cm−1. The result corresponded to the oxidase-like property of three samples. In conclusion, the results of the above analyses indicated that the oxidase-like activity of MnNCD could be modulated by changing the dosage of EDA. The modulating effect of EDA dosage was not related to N doping, which was mainly driven by the synergistic effect between the alkaline environment and the harsh reaction conditions. The former could provide more O-based groups for the coordination of Mn species by promoting the depolymerization of CMC. The latter could enhance the opportunity for N-based and O-based groups to coordinate with Mn species (Fig. 3g). In addition, in order to balance the fluorescence property and oxidase-like activity, the MnNCD2-24 material was employed for the following study.

The effects of reaction time on the oxidase-like activity of MnNCD were further verified. As shown in Fig. 3d, the catalytic ability of MnNCD2-12, MnNCD2-24, and MnNCD2-36 increased by prolonging the hydrothermal reaction time. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of these three MnNCD (Fig. S3b) firstly showed an enhancing trend with increasing hydrothermal time from 12 to 24 h and then a decreasing trend with further prolonging hydrothermal time to 36 h. The XRD patterns (Fig. 3e) of three MnNCD showed a broad peak ascribing to the graphitic carbon (002) crystal plane. The calculated d002 for MnNCD2-12, MnNCD2-24, and MnNCD2-36 were 0.359 nm, 0.356 nm, and 0.340 nm, respectively. Such results confirmed that the long duration of the hydrothermal process would generate harsher reaction conditions and thus reduce the graphite layer spacing of the induced carbon structure.

As shown in Fig. 3f, the intensity of the peak at 565 cm−1, corresponding to Mn-O/Mn-N, increased as the hydrothermal time increased from 12 h to 36 h, which was consistent with the results of oxidase-like activity. As shown in Fig. 5 , the content of O element in MnNCD2-12, MnNCD2-24, and MnNCD2-36 was 17.5 %, 15.93 %, and 13.37 %, respectively. The results showed that sustained dehydration reaction reduced the content of O element in MnNCD with increasing hydrothermal time. Meanwhile, the content of the N element in MnNCD gradually increased to 13.06 %. The content of the Mn element in the three samples was 0.50 %, 0.40 %, and 0.83 %, respectively, and did not increase with prolonging the hydrothermal time. However, in the high-resolution spectra of O 1 s and N 1 s, the content of Mn-O and Mn-N bonds increased with time extension, which was consistent with the trend of oxidase activity of MnNCD.

Therefore, harsh hydrothermal conditions could modulate the oxidase-like activity of MnNCD by controlling the amount of Mn doping and the coordination environment (Fig. 3g). In addition, as the reaction conditions became harsher, more amino groups were converted to pyridine N, pyrrole N and graphite N, which could also contribute to the increased oxidase-like activity of MnNCD (Kang et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2021a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and oxidase-like activity performance of MnNCD2-36
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity and oxidase-like activity of CDs were susceptible to a variety of factors (e.g., CD concentration, solution pH, temperature). Therefore, the fluorescence property and oxidase-like activity of MnNCD2-36 were further discussed, and the catalytic mechanism of MnNCD2-36 for TMB was elaborated. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S5a) of MnNCD2-36 exhibited two absorption peaks at 230 nm and 350 nm, respectively. The former is attributed to the π − π* transition of CC, and the latter is caused by the n − π* transition of O H and CO/CN groups on the surface of CDs (Guo et al., 2023b). When the excitation wavelength was constant at 360 nm, the MnNCD2-36 displayed a blue emission spectrum centered at 460 nm. Similar to most of the biomass-based CDs, the emission wavelength of MnNCD2-36 exhibited excitation-dependent characteristics (Fig. S5b) (Si et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2021b; Xu et al., 2021). This behavior could be associated with the heterogeneous size distribution of CDs. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of MnNCD2-36 showed concentration dependence behavior (Fig. S5c). The MnNCD2-36 had the highest fluorescence intensity when its concentration in water was 0.12 mg/mL. As the concentration continued increasing to 0.20 mg/mL, the fluorescence intensity of MnNCD2-36 was reduced due to the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) feature. The photoluminescence property of MnNCD2-36 was also affected by the pH environment, as shown in Fig. S6. Compared with neutral or alkaline pH, MnNCD2-36 had stronger fluorescence in an acidic environment, especially with a pH value of 3–4. This result may be due to the fact that the carboxyl group of MnNCD2-36 leads to fluorescence quenching in strongly acidic and alkaline environments, which was consistent with previous studies (Liu et al., 2020).

To fully utilize the oxidase-like activity of MnNCD2-36, the effect of pH values of buffer solutions on the ability of MnNCD2-36 to catalyze the substrate TMB was explored, and the result is shown in Fig. 6 a. The solution of MnNCD2-36 incubated with TMB for 2 min had the maximum UV–Vis absorption at pH of 4. Interestingly, MnNCD2-36 had both excellent fluorescence intensity and oxidase-like activity at pH of 4, which was beneficial for the sensing detection of CDs in an acidic environment. The catalytic mechanism was revealed by the EPR spectrum (Fig. 6b), confirming free radicals were generated during the incubation of MnNCD2-36 with TMB. The result showed that a typical signal attributed to superoxide anion radicals was captured in the presence of MnNCD2-36 (Wang et al., 2024). Fig. 6c illustrates a schematic diagram of MnNCD2-36 catalyzed TMB. According to previous literature (Feng et al., 2023; He et al., 2024), the dissolved oxygen (O2) initially accepted an electron from the Mn active center on the surface of the CDs. It was thus transformed into superoxide radicals (•O2 −), while the Mn2+ was oxidized into Mn3+ in accompany. As a common catalytic substrate, TMB had a benzidine structure that contained two readily oxidized amino groups. After reacting with •O2 −, TMB lost an electron and was converted into the oxidized TMB+, accompanied by the color change from colorless to blue. The •O2 − accepted the electron and was eventually reduced into H2O. To further evaluate the oxidase-like activity of MnNCD2-36, the typical Michaelis-Menten curve and Lineweaver-Burk plot (Fig. 6d and 6e) were obtained by varying the concentration of TMB. The Km and Vmax were 0.159 mM and 19.8 × 10-5 mM s−1, respectively.

Table 1 demonstrates the comparison of the MnNCD2-36 in this work with CDs from the literature in terms of raw materials, application conditions, catalytic rate, and substrate affinity. CDs with oxidase-like activity can be divided into metal-doped CDs and non-metal-doped CDs types. The latter required UV or visible light activation to produce oxidase-like activity. The Vmax of MnNCD2-36 was much higher than the Vmax (0.6 × 10-5 ∼ 2.74 × 10-5) of non-metal-doped CDs. The oxidase-like activity of MnNCD2-36 does not require light activation. In addition, the Vmax of MnNCD2-36 was still dominant compared to metal-doped CDs. Surprisingly, the Km value of MnNCD2-36 was lower than the Km (0.168 ∼ 3.040) of all reported CDs, indicating the excellent affinity of MnNCD2-36 with TMB. It was worth mentioning that MnNCD in this work was more sustainable with renewable biomass as a precursor compared to other CDs with oxidase-like activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction of nitrate and MnNCD2-36 + TMB mixed solution
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the excellent fluorescent property and oxidase-like activity, MnNCD2-36 was used as a sensor to detect nitrite. After introducing nitrite in the MnNCD2-36 and TMB mixed solution (MnNCD2-36 + TMB system), the interaction between them caused the solution to change from blue to green. Meanwhile, the UV–Vis absorbance band at 652 nm decreased, while the absorbance at 445 nm significantly increased (Fig. 7 a). The above phenomenon could be explained by the diazotization reaction of NO2 – with aromatic primary amines of TMB+, resulting in a solution color change and UV–Vis absorption signal (Wang et al., 2021c) (Fig. 7b). In addition, the fluorescence intensity of the MnNCD2-36 + TMB system was significantly decreased when the nitrite was introduced (Fig. 7c). Fig. 7d showed that the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of diazotized TMB+ overlapped the fluorescence emission spectrum of MnNCD2-36 at 435 nm. The higher the concentration of nitrite, the larger the overlap area could be generated. It was hypothesized that the mechanism of diazotized TMB+ quenching the fluorescence of MnNCD2-36 was fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE). The fluorescence lifetimes before and after the addition of nitrite were compared to validate the quenching mechanism further. As shown in Fig. 7e, the fluorescence lifetime of MnNCD2-36 was not significantly altered after introducing nitrite, indicating that IFE caused the fluorescence quenching induced by diazotized TMB+. The schematic of the quenching process is depicted in Fig. 7f. Without the addition of nitrite, MnNCD2-36 emitted blue fluorescence under the irradiation of the UV lamp. After the addition of nitrite, the blue fluorescence was absorbed by the diazotized TMB+ due to the overlap of the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the obtained diazotized TMB+ with the fluorescence emission spectrum of MnNCD2-36, and thus the fluorescence was quenched. In conclusion, the dual variation of visible color and fluorescence intensity of the MnNCD2-36 + TMB system provided feasibility for the development of a bimodal ratiometric colorimetric/fluorescence detection platform for nitrite based on biomass-derived CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of a colorimetric/fluorescent dual-mode sensor for nitrite detection
ce:section.ce:para: With the above verification, the simultaneous change in visible color and fluorescence intensity made MnNCD2-36 as a promising sensor for bimodal ratiometric colorimetric/fluorescence detection of nitrite. Fig. S6 demonstrates the curves of the absorbance ratios of diazotized TMB at 652 nm and 445 nm with reaction times. The results showed that the absorbance ratio was gradually stable at the reaction time of 15 min. Subsequently, a bimodal ratiometric colorimetric analysis of gradient concentrations of NO2 – was performed using the MnNCD2-36 + TMB system. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the absorbance of UV–Vis at 445 nm enhanced with increasing nitrite concentration, while the absorbance at 652 nm decreased slightly. Correspondingly, the visual color of the solution changed from blue to green at low concentrations of nitrite and to yellow at high concentrations of nitrite (Fig. S7). The absorbance ratio of A652/A445 (Fig. 8b) showed a well-linear relationship with the logarithm of NO2 – concentration in the range of 2.5–200 μM, and the linear fitting equation was y = -1.604x + 3.848 (R2 = 0.994). According to the principle of triple signal-to-noise ratio (S/N = 3), the detection limit of the bimodal ratiometric colorimetric method was 0.189 μM. Subsequently, the affinity of the fluorescence method to detect nitrite was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 8c, the fluorescence intensity of the MnNCD2-36 + TMB system gradually decreased with increasing nitrite concentration. The linear regression equation for the nitrite concentration in the range of 2.5–100 μM was expressed as y = 0.019x + 1.131 (Fig. 8d), and the detection limit was 1.060 μM based on S/N = 3. The established MnNCD2-36 + TMB system had the advantages of high sensitivity, wide linear range, and low detection limit in the detection of nitrite, which can be compared with other nitrite detection methods (Table. S2).

In practice, selectivity and anti-interference ability are important indicators for evaluating the bimodal ratiometric colorimetric/fluorescence detection strategy. Fig. 8e and 8f showed that, before the introduction of nitrite, the MnNCD2-36 + TMB system exhibited negligible responses to common metal ions and anions. After the addition of nitrite, the absorbance ratio and fluorescence intensity of the MnNCD2-36 + TMB system decreased rapidly. The results indicated that the MnNCD2-36 + TMB system had excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability for nitrite detection, which might be attributed to the specific diazotization reaction of TMB with NO2 –. In addition, the accuracy of the dual-mode detection method was evaluated during the detection of real samples, e.g., luncheon meat, sausage, and salted duck eggs. Table. S3 showed that the recovery rates of the bimodal ratiometric colorimetric/fluorescence detection method for nitrite by the MnNCD2-36 + TMB system were 95.6–108.2 % and 109.4–118.2 %, respectively. The results indicated that this dual-mode assay had high stability and accurate results and could be applied to the analysis of nitrite in complex matrices.

MnNCD with oxidase-like activity and fluorescent property was successfully prepared from CMC for the first time. The polymer structure and strong coordination ability of CMC contribute to the good dispersion of Mn species during the hydrothermal process. The Mn-O and Mn-N bonds were formed by the coordination of O-based and N-based groups with Mn species, which endowed MnNCD with excellent oxidase-like activity. The content of these two bonds could be regulated by the content of EDA and hydrothermal time. The higher EDA dosage and hydrothermal time prompted more formation of Mn-O and Mn-N bonds in MnNCD, which enhanced the enzymatic activity of MnNCD. The obtained MnNCD2-36 had an efficient catalytic affinity for TMB with Vmax = 19.80 × 10-5 mM s−1 and Km = 0.160 mM. Finally, a ratiometric colorimetric/fluorescence dual-mode detection platform for different concentrations of nitrite was successfully established. Due to the specificity of the diazotization reaction and the dual-signal output mode, the MnNCD2-36 + TMB system had excellent sensitivity, accuracy, and anti-interference for the detection of nitrite. The preparation of MnNCD with high catalytic activity in this work contributed to the development of a dual-mode nitrite detection platform. It could inspire more efforts to explore the application of CD nanozymes in the fields of sensing.
